findings.description: Dedup key is only msg.to (recipient address), so any two distinct messages to the same recipient result in the second being silently dropped and counted as skipped; subject and body are ignored, making the dedup semantically wrong for multi-message-per-recipient use cases.

smtplib.SMTP() constructor is outside the try block; if it raises (network error, DNS failure), the exception propagates and aborts the entire loop, leaving _pending uncleared so a subsequent flush() will re-attempt all messages including any already successfully sent in prior loop iterations.

On sendmail() failure the exception escapes the loop, _pending.clear() on line 65 is never reached, and a subsequent flush() call will re-send messages already delivered in earlier loop iterations (double-send), while no error is logged and the failure is completely silent.

_seen is never cleared in flush(), so after a successful flush any re-enqueue attempt for the same recipient address is permanently silently dropped as a duplicate across all future batches until the caller explicitly calls clear_dedup_cache().

clear_dedup_cache() clears _seen but not _pending, so a caller can re-enqueue a recipient that is already in _pending (not yet flushed), producing a duplicate _pending entry that causes double-send on the next flush().

_render() builds a raw RFC 2822 message string without encoding non-ASCII characters in subject or body per RFC 2047, which will silently produce a malformed message if msg.subject or msg.body contains non-ASCII content, and behavior depends entirely on SMTP server leniency.
A new SMTP TCP connection is opened and closed per message inside the loop; for N messages this incurs N full handshake round-trips; noted briefly per scope — defer detail to critic-performance.
